She says, “I understand that I am losing brain cells at my age, but I would still like to keep my mind and body active. ” Which is the best response to Alice? a. “You are correct that you cannot form new brain cells at your age but you should continue with your activities because they offer excellent physical and mental health benefits and in turn will lower your risk for dementi

choices.B: “ Although most brain development occurs early in life, we still form some new brain cells in a couple of areas of the brain during adulthood. You should continue with your activities because they offer excellent physical and mental health benefits and are neuroprotective. ”
choices.C: “Scientists now know that we do continue to form new brain cells throughout the entire brain during adulthoo
choices.D: Continue with your activities because you are producing new brain cells in the frontal lobe and this will decrease your risk of dementia. ”d. “You should continue the social activities such as bridge, volunteering, and the book club but should consider the risks and benefits of physical activities such as dancing. If you were to fall and break a hip, this could lead to prolonged hospitalization, loss of independence, and ultimately increase your risk of dementia. ”82.

cards.explanation: While it was once thought that brain neurons did not regenerate, we now known that while most brain development occurs early in life, we continue to form some new brain cells throughout life. As we age, we need to engage in activities that keep our brains healthy by encouraging this growth. Examples are diet, exercise, socialization, and cognitive stimulation.

cards.question: What is the best treatment for AIDS dementia complex?
cards.explanation: All persons with AIDS should be treated with antiretroviral therapy. Those who develop dementia complex should have those symptoms treated with appropriate pharmacological or nonpharmacological interventions.

cards.question: As a PMHNP working on the consult liaison team, you know the importance of preventing delirium due to which of the following?

choices.B: Risk of harm to the client and staff

cards.explanation: Studies have identified high rates of mortality post hospitalization for delirium so the best treatment is prevention.

cards.question: When working with a 26-year-old, Mike, who presents for treatment of cannabis use and gambling, you use motivational interviewing techniques. As a PMHNP , you are familiar with the core counseling skills used in motivational interviewing. Mike made the following state-ment: “I don’t know why I came here in the first place but I thought maybe some medica-tion would help me. ” You respond by saying, “You’re feeling confused about the process” and Mike replies, “I never thought I’d need to come to a place like this. ” You respond, “You kept your appointment today and I appreciate the courage it took for you to come here. ” What two motivational interviewing techniques are used in this interaction?
cards.explanation: When a person is in contemplation stage, interventions should be affirming and reflecting.

cards.question: You are a PMHNP working in a hospitalist role on an acute inpatient psychiatric unit at a lo-cal hospital. As you make rounds, the registered nurse informs you that a 32-year-old client who was admitted for alcohol detox has a score of 17 on the Clinical Institute Withdrawal Assessment for Alcohol. What phase of withdrawal is this client in?
cards.explanation: CIWA cut off scores are as follows: 0–9, absent or very mild withdrawal; 10–15, mild withdrawal; 16–20, moderate withdrawal; and 21–67 , severe withdrawal.

cards.question: A client who has been addicted to opioids has not used in 15 days. During your medication management visit, the client states, “I’m going to die from not having my Opanas. You need to give me something now. ” The PMHNP’s best response is:

choices.A: “I know you are feeling very uncomfortable and we need to get you to the emergency room immediately to prevent a seizure. ”
choices.B: “I know you are feeling very uncomfortable, let’s take your vital signs and talk about a trial on Catapres to treat your withdrawal symptoms. ”
choices.C: “You have been using Opana for a long time and it is going to take several months for the withdrawal to en
choices.D: In the meantime, I will see you weekly. ”d. “There is no treatment for opioid withdrawal; you will have to wait it out. ”87 .

cards.explanation: Opioid withdrawal symptoms can be treated with central alpha agonists.

cards.question: Signs and symptoms of cannabis intoxication include:
cards.explanation: Persons intoxicated on cannabis exhibit distorted perceptions, increase relaxation and sensitivity, and loss of coordination.

cards.question: Which defense mechanisms are commonly used by persons with obsessive–compulsive personality disorder?
cards.explanation: Persons with obsessive–compulsive personality disorder use defense mechanisms of rationalization, isolation of affect, and intellectualization to make sense of their behavior. 415REVIEW QUESTION ANSWERS

cards.question: You have been working with Cody, a 30-year-old single man, in weekly individual psychother-apy for 3 weeks. At the start of session 4 he says, “I noticed when I came in that your usual parking spot has a new car in it with temporary tags, and it’s a BMW. Nice car. ” What is the best response from the PMHNP psychotherapist to Cody?

choices.A: “Thanks for noticing, it is a nice car. ”
choices.B: “How do you know what spot I park in?”
choices.C: “I noticed you drive a BMW as well, how do you like your car?”
choices.D: “Sounds like having expensive things is important to you. ” 90.

cards.explanation: In a therapy relationship, the therapist should try to understand the meaning of a client’s statement rather than engage in social conversations.

cards.question: When working in individual psychotherapy with a client who has a personality disorder, what are the primary treatment goals?

choices.A: Change the client’s personality structure and make him or her more adaptable in every-day life.
choices.B: Reparent the client, following Bowlby’s theoretical framework.
choices.C: Allow the client to reprocess his or her childhood trauma because all clients with per-sonality disorders have a history of severe abuse.
choices.D: Assist the client in changing dysfunctional interpersonal relationships and use of imma-ture defense mechanisms.91 .

cards.explanation: Persons with personality disorders have a pervasive maladaptive pattern of behavior and the goal of therapy is to slowly shift how they relate in the world and begin to use higher-order defenses.

cards.question: For a client who has paranoid personality disorder, what are the best treatment strategies?

choices.A: Confront negative and misinterpreted thoughts and feelings.
choices.C: Engage the client in detailed and emotional responses and dialog.
choices.D: Do not challenge negative views or recollections of events.92.

cards.explanation: Persons with fixed false beliefs should not be challenged.

cards.question: John, a client with paranoid personality disorder, states the following: “I noticed there is a red light in the upper corner of your door and it has been going on and off during our ses-sions. Are you recording me?” What is the PMHNP best response?

choices.A: “No, it would be illegal for me to record you, and that is not a camera it’s just a red light. ”
choices.B: “John, thank you for asking the question. The light you see in the upper corner of my door tells me when a client has arrived and is in the waiting room. The client turns on the light, as you do, when they arrive in the waiting room, alerting me their arrival, and I turn off the light when we get into the office using the switch by my desk. ”
choices.C: “Come on John, do you think I would record your sessions? You are not that interesting. I’m just kidding, no John, it is not a recorder or camera. ”
choices.D: “John, it takes courage to ask me the question. Tell me a time in your life when you had a similar experience. ”93.

cards.explanation: When working with a paranoid client, help the person find proof of meaning and explain any questions in a matter-of-fact manner.

cards.question: As a PMHNP , you understand the genetic factors that contribute to psychiatric and personal-ity disorders. Persons who develop antisocial personality disorder often are raised in families with high rates of which of the following?
cards.explanation: Being raised in an alcoholic family increases the likelihood of chaos, unpredictability, and lack of rules and order, leading to higher rates of develop-ing antisocial personality disorder.

cards.question: Persons with obsessive–compulsive personality disorder often use isolation as a de-fense mechanism. Which of the following examples best describes isolation as a defense mechanism?

choices.A: Staying in the house for days or weeks to clean
choices.B: Declining invites by friends or family to attend social gatherings
choices.C: Describing information with very little affect variation
choices.D: Being an introvert in the work setting to prevent talking with coworkers95.

cards.explanation: Isolation is a defense mechanism often used by people with obsessive–compulsive personality disorder and has to do with affect and emotion rather than getting out and being social.

cards.question: In the Diagnostic and Statistical Manual of Mental Disorders, 5th Edition (DSM-5), how should a personality disorder be coded?

choices.A: Coded with the major psychiatric disorder

cards.explanation: DSM-5 no longer uses the axial system.

cards.question: When prescribing a selective serotonin reuptake inhibitor (SSRI) for a child or young adult up to age 24, what education must be included?

choices.A: Black box warning about increased suicidality in this population
choices.B: Black box warning about increased risk of mania in this population
choices.C: Risk of sexual side effects on this class of medication
choices.D: Risk of stomach upset and headaches, to prevent unnecessary primary care visits97 .

cards.explanation: The SSRIs all carry a black box warning for increased suicidal ideation for this age group.

cards.question: Tommy is an 8 year-old who presents to the PMHNP for evaluation of attention-deficit hyper-activity disorder. His mother completed the Vanderbilt ADHD rating scale and brought in the Vanderbilt teaching rating scale. Both your clinical interview and the rating scales indicate Tommy has ADHD. What assessment indicator(s) need to be completed prior to starting a stimulant mediation?

choices.A: Get a copy of the rating scale completed by his grandparents.
choices.B: Assess for family history of cardiovascular disease and, if positive for conduction prob-lems, order an electrocardiogram before prescribing medication.
choices.C: Obtain blood pressure, and pulse, and begin the stimulant medication.
choices.D: Assess for a family history of bipolar disorder. 98.

cards.explanation: American Academy of Child and Adolescent Psychiatry practice parameters require physical exam, pulse, weight, height, and blood pressure workup prior to the start of stimulant medication. Because his grandfather had a cardiac conduction problem, an electrocardiogram (ECG) should also be obtained prior to the start of medication.

cards.question: A mother brings in her 7-year-old son for a psychiatric follow-up visit with the PMHNP . This is the fourth visit the PMHNP has had with the client, his mother, and his younger sister, Renee, now 7 months old. You notice that she has a decrease in head growth, along with stereotypic motions of the hands, often licking and slapping. Renee has also lost her lan-guage skills. What medical condition do you suspect Renee has developed?
cards.explanation: Girls with Rett’s syndrome develop normally and around the 7th month regress, with a decrease in head size and language loss.

cards.question: You are treating Timothy, a 16-year-old boy, for an eating disorder. Timothy is of normal weight and socially extroverted, at times appearing to seek attention when in a peer group or class. Timothy’s symptoms are most consistent with which eating disorder?
cards.explanation: Clients with bulimia are often of normal weight or overweight and are outgoing.

cards.question: You are treating a 14-year-old female for attention-deficit hyperactivity disorder (ADHD) who has a family history of bipolar disorder. As a PMHNP familiar with symptoms of both ADHD and pediatric bipolar disorder, you know the following are overlapping symptoms of both disorders:

choices.A: Excessive talking, increased activity, and distractibility
choices.B: Irritability, sleep problems, and mood swings
choices.C: Excessive talking, irritability, and sleep problems
choices.D: Sleep problems, mood swings, and distractibility

cards.explanation: Clients with ADHD and bipolar disorder often have excessive talking, increased activity, and distractibility.
